---
title: "About"
---

I came to data science slowly, and mostly by accident. I studied psychology at UC Santa Barbara, and while I was fascinated by how people make decisions, I left school without much of a quantitative foundation. A math post-bacc at CUNY a few years later helped me start to close that gap, and I've been filling it in ever since — mostly on the job, learning from people who were further along than me. Whatever I know about using data in a business I picked up one role at a time, and I still feel like I'm learning the craft.

## Starting in Support

My first real exposure to business data was in support at ZocDoc. I wasn't doing analytics — I was answering tickets. But after enough conversations with users, you start to notice that the same issues cluster in ways that aren't always visible upstream. I started pulling ticket data into spreadsheets and sharing the patterns with the product team. It wasn't modeling; it was just showing up with a clearer picture of what customers were running into. Looking back, that's probably the thing that stuck with me most — start with what's actually happening, then figure out what to do about it.

## Product Specialist at Square

I joined Square as a product specialist during the hardware transition from swipe to chip to contactless readers. The job was effectively bridging support and product — sitting with the CS org, trying to understand where customers were getting stuck, and translating that into signals the product team could act on. Over five years I gradually grew into an analytics role. The piece of work I'm fondest of from that time was a classifier that helped automate some of the support case tagging and freed up some CS capacity. But more than any single project, Square is where I started to appreciate how much of the value sits at the seam between support and product — where the data is messy, the stakeholders have different goals, and the job is really just making sure the right evidence reaches the right decision.

I also taught a data analytics course at General Assembly in 2017 while I was at Square — 10 weeks of SQL, statistics, and experimentation with career changers. Teaching a room full of people who are seeing this stuff for the first time is the fastest way to find out whether you actually understand it yourself.

## Building DS Chops at Atlassian

I joined Atlassian in 2020 as the first data scientist hire for the events team. It was a good place for me to start — the scope was contained enough that I could learn by building the pipeline end-to-end (instrumentation, measurement, experimentation) rather than inheriting a stack I didn't yet know how to reason about. From there I moved into marketing data science, where the problems got bigger: Ideal Customer Profile work, buying group analysis, propensity scoring for the enterprise GTM org. A lot of what I was doing was carrying forward the same seam-between-functions work I'd learned at Square, but now with real modeling tools behind it — and a lot more room to get things wrong.

Toward the end of my time there I spent a lot of time thinking about how LLMs might change the shape of DS work itself. My honest read is that they're already a meaningful unlock — they compress the boring parts of the loop (querying, scaffolding, summarizing) so more time can go toward the judgment calls that actually drive recommendations. But the unlock only holds if you pair it with the verification today's models still require: checking the join, sanity-checking the numbers, pressure-testing the framing before it goes to a stakeholder. I've seen the tooling work really well in practice, and I've also seen it quietly produce work that looks right and isn't. I'd rather err on the slow side until the tooling catches up.

A lot of what I ended up doing at Atlassian was helping the rest of the data org find that balance — setting up MCP connections, writing knowledge files, sitting with people through their first few workflows, and trying to be honest about what still needed a human in the loop. That work became the adoption playbook I've written up separately, and it's one of the parts of the last few years I've enjoyed most. It pulled on the same thing I liked about teaching at General Assembly — watching someone go from "I don't know how this works" to using it comfortably is a pretty hard feeling to beat.

## Where I Am Now

I was laid off from Atlassian in March 2026 as part of a broader restructure. I'm based in San Francisco and looking for senior data science roles — ideally ones where the work is close to the business, where the progression from support to product to DS can be an asset rather than something to explain away, and where I can keep learning how AI tooling actually changes this kind of work in practice.

## Education

- **B.A. Psychology** — UC Santa Barbara
- **Post-Baccalaureate, Mathematics** — City University of New York
